curiosities.body: The first computer "bug" was a real moth, taped into a logbook in 1947.
HTTP 418 "I'm a teapot" is a real status code, from a 1998 April Fools RFC.
Tony Hoare called inventing the null reference his "billion-dollar mistake."
The @ in email? Ray Tomlinson grabbed it in 1971 because the key was unused.
Python is named after Monty Python — not the snake.
Linus Torvalds wrote the first working version of Git in about ten days, in 2005.
Signed 32-bit Unix time runs out on Jan 19, 2038 — the Y2038 problem.
The first webcam watched a coffee pot, so Cambridge staff wouldn't find it empty.
grep is named after the old ed command g/re/p — global / regex / print.
The placeholders "foo" and "bar" likely come from WWII-era slang "FUBAR."
Ada Lovelace published the first algorithm meant for a machine — in 1843.
Your whole 127.0.0.0/8 block — 16 million addresses — is loopback. You use one.
Git relied on SHA-1 until Google produced a real collision, "SHAttered," in 2017.
A background "daemon" is named after Maxwell's demon — not the evil kind.
"Byte" was spelled with a y on purpose, so no one would confuse it with "bit."
The Apollo 11 guidance computer landed on the Moon with about 4 KB of RAM.
Margaret Hamilton coined "software engineering" while writing Apollo's flight code.
On paper tape you deleted a character by punching every hole — that's why DEL is 127.
Grace Hopper wrote the first compiler, the A-0, in 1952.
"Computer" was originally a job title — a person who did calculations by hand.
The first website is still online at info.cern.ch, barely changed since the early '90s.
Tim Berners-Lee's first browser was also an editor — the web was meant to be writable.
IBM's first gigabyte drive (1980) weighed over 200 kg and cost a fortune.
Douglas Engelbart demoed the mouse, hypertext, and video calls — back in 1968.
QWERTY was arranged in the 1870s to keep typewriter bars from jamming.
The word "robot" comes from a 1920 Czech play; "robota" means forced labor.
"Bit" is short for "binary digit," a term coined back in 1947.
Hedy Lamarr co-patented frequency hopping — an ancestor of Wi-Fi and Bluetooth.
The first spam email was sent over ARPANET in 1978.
The Morris Worm (1988) was one of the first internet worms — and the first CFAA case.
PHP originally stood for "Personal Home Page."
Brendan Eich created JavaScript in about ten days, in 1995.
JavaScript is unrelated to Java — the name was pure marketing.
Unix was first written in assembly, then rewritten in C around 1973.
C and Unix grew up together at Bell Labs, both shaped by Dennis Ritchie.
"Hello, world" traces back to a Bell Labs tutorial from 1972.
SQL was first called SEQUEL, until a trademark forced the rename.
GIF's creator insists it's pronounced "jif." The argument never ends.
Wi-Fi doesn't actually stand for anything — it's just a brand name.
ping was named after the sound of sonar, by its author in 1983.
"Spam" for junk messages comes straight from a Monty Python sketch.
A TCP connection opens with a three-way handshake: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK.
UDP makes no promises about delivery or order — that's the whole point.
IPv4 has ~4.3 billion addresses, and we used them all up. Hence IPv6.
IPv6 has enough addresses to label every grain of sand many times over.
A MAC address's first half identifies the hardware's manufacturer.
HTTP 4xx means "your fault"; 5xx means "the server's fault."
traceroute works by deliberately letting packets expire one hop at a time.
sudo is just short for "superuser do."
awk is named after its authors: Aho, Weinberger, and Kernighan.
Anything written to /dev/null vanishes — it's the system's bit bucket.
In Unix, almost everything is a file — even devices and network sockets.
The ~ for "home" survives from a terminal where Home and ~ shared one key.
vi was written by Bill Joy in 1976; "how do I quit vim" is eternal.
Ctrl+C sends SIGINT — and the keystroke itself is ASCII character 3.
cron takes its name from the Greek word for time, "chronos."
tar means "tape archive" — it's older than the disks you back up with it.
A "tty" is a teletypewriter; terminals are named after machines they replaced.
The root user is simply UID 0 — that number is what makes it all-powerful.
Lisp (1958) is the second-oldest language still in use, after Fortran.
0.1 + 0.2 doesn't equal 0.3 in most languages — blame binary floating point.
Booleans are named after George Boole, a mathematician born in 1815.
"Turing complete" traces to Alan Turing's imaginary machine from 1936.
Regular expressions grew out of Stephen Kleene's 1950s math on languages.
The semaphore in concurrency was named by Dijkstra, after railway signals.
Dijkstra designed his famous shortest-path algorithm in about 20 minutes.
Quicksort was invented by Tony Hoare around 1959.
The first antivirus, "Reaper," was built to delete an earlier program, "Creeper."
Public-key crypto was secretly invented at GCHQ years before it went public.
Turing's team at Bletchley Park cracked Enigma, helping shorten WWII.
Salting passwords means identical passwords still hash to different values.
For years, the world's most common password was literally "123456."
Tux the Linux penguin exists because Linus likes penguins — one even bit him.
Moore's Law was a 1965 observation, not a law of physics.
"Google" is a misspelling of "googol" — the number 10 to the 100th.
The QR code was invented in 1994 to track car parts.
Bluetooth is named after a 10th-century Danish king; its logo is his runic initials.
The power symbol is a 1 inside a 0 — binary for on and off.
The Y2K bug came from storing years as two digits to save scarce memory.
The "404 = a room at CERN" story is a myth; it's just an HTTP status code.
Cookies are named after "magic cookies," a Unix idea older than the web.
Ports only go up to 65535 because the port field is 16 bits wide.
On Unix, ports below 1024 need root — a rule from the early days of the net.
The bell character (\a) can still make a terminal beep out loud.
A 502 error means the gateway's upstream failed — not your front end.
RAM is "volatile" because it forgets everything the moment power is cut.
Before "at," the @ symbol had a dozen quirky names across languages.
Kernighan and Ritchie's "K&R" C book (1978) still shapes how we write code.
Hexadecimal needs 16 symbols, so it borrows A–F after 0–9.
There are two hard things in CS: cache invalidation, naming, and off-by-one errors.

sponsored.body: Aside is open source and privacy-first — it never reads your code.
Enjoying these? Aside drops a fresh dev curiosity into every wait-state.
Aside runs locally and works offline — the network never blocks your terminal.
Aside reads two harmless fields and throws the rest away. Run `aside about`.
Like a fact? Every Aside line is signed and ready to screenshot.
Aside never touches your files, paths, or prompts — and the code proves it.
Want your message in dev's most-watched line? Sponsor Aside.
One curious line while the AI thinks. Aside, MIT-licensed.
New here? npm i -g @aside/cli && aside install.
Aside turns idle wait-states into something worth reading.
